title: Introduction to ppforest2
text: Train projection-pursuit tree iris dataset: tree splits linear projections features. Use summary() see variable importance: Predict new observations:
code: library(ppforest2) #>  #> Attaching package: 'ppforest2' #> The following object is masked from 'package:datasets': #>  #>     iris  tree <- pptr(Type ~ ., data = iris, seed = 42) tree #>  #> Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree: #> If ([ 0.19 0.71 -0.66 -0.17 ] * x) < 1.070804: #>  If ([ 0.23 0.36 -0.44 -0.79 ] * x) < -1.062904: #>    Predict: virginica  #>  Else: #>    Predict: versicolor  #> Else: #>   Predict: setosa summary(tree) #>  #> Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree #> ------------------------------------- #> 150 observations of 4 features #> Regularization parameter: 0  #> Classes: #>  setosa #>  versicolor #>  virginica  #> Formula: #>  Type ~ Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width + Petal.Length + Petal.Width -      1  #> ------------------------------------- #> Variable Importance: #>  #>       Variable         σ Projection #> 1 Petal.Length 1.7652982  0.7787341 #> 2  Petal.Width 0.7622377  0.3437867 #> 3  Sepal.Width 0.4358663  0.1806545 #> 4 Sepal.Length 0.8280661  0.1471821 #>  #> Note: Variable importance was calculated using scaled coefficients (|a_j| * σ_j). #> Variable contributions can only be theoretically interpreted as such #> if the model was trained on scaled data. Scaling also changes the #> projection-pursuit optimization, which may affect the resulting tree. #> ------------------------------------- #> Confusion Matrix: #> TODO preds <- predict(tree, iris) table(predicted = preds, actual = iris$Type) #>             actual #> predicted    setosa versicolor virginica #>   setosa         50          0         0 #>   versicolor      0         48         1 #>   virginica       0          2        49

title: Introduction to ppforest2
text: Train forest 100 trees, considering 2 randomly chosen variables split: summary shows OOB (--bag) error estimate three variable importance measures: Projection: average absolute projection coefficient across splits Weighted: projection importance weighted number observations routed node Permuted: decrease OOB accuracy variable permuted Predict class probabilities:
code: forest <- pprf(Type ~ ., data = iris, size = 100, n_vars = 2, seed = 42) summary(forest) #>  #> Random Forest of Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree #> ------------------------------------- #> Size: 100 trees #> 150 observations of 4 features #> Regularization parameter: 0  #> Classes: #>  setosa #>  versicolor #>  virginica  #> Formula: #>  Type ~ Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width + Petal.Length + Petal.Width -      1  #> OOB error: 4 % #> ------------------------------------- #> Variable Importance: #>  #>       Variable         σ Projection   Weighted   Permuted #> 1 Petal.Length 1.7652982 0.55146503 0.50911570 0.30612773 #> 2  Petal.Width 0.7622377 0.28662407 0.23666532 0.26514626 #> 3 Sepal.Length 0.8280661 0.14083074 0.09399321 0.07176236 #> 4  Sepal.Width 0.4358663 0.08313646 0.07558592 0.05124974 #>  #> Note: Variable importance was calculated using scaled coefficients (|a_j| * σ_j). #> Variable contributions can only be theoretically interpreted as such #> if the model was trained on scaled data. Scaling also changes the #> projection-pursuit optimization, which may affect the resulting tree. #> ------------------------------------- #> Confusion Matrix: #> TODO probs <- predict(forest, iris[1:5, ], type = "prob") probs #>   setosa versicolor virginica #> 1      1          0         0 #> 2      1          0         0 #> 3      1          0         0 #> 4      1          0         0 #> 5      1          0         0

title: Introduction to ppforest2
text: ppforest2 provides several plot types via plot() (requires ggplot2):     forests, specify tree_index plot individual tree:
code: # Tree structure with projected data histograms at each node plot(tree, type = "structure") # Variable importance bar chart plot(tree, type = "importance") # Data projected onto the first split's projection vector plot(tree, type = "projection") # Decision boundaries for two selected variables plot(tree, type = "boundaries") plot(forest, type = "structure", tree_index = 1)

title: Introduction to ppforest2
text: Set lambda > 0 use Penalized Discriminant Analysis instead LDA. can help features highly correlated:
code: tree_pda <- pptr(Type ~ ., data = iris, lambda = 0.5, seed = 42) summary(tree_pda) #>  #> Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree #> ------------------------------------- #> 150 observations of 4 features #> Regularization parameter: 0.5  #> Classes: #>  setosa #>  versicolor #>  virginica  #> Formula: #>  Type ~ Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width + Petal.Length + Petal.Width -      1  #> ------------------------------------- #> Variable Importance: #>  #>       Variable         σ Projection #> 1 Petal.Length 1.7652982 0.56312275 #> 2  Petal.Width 0.7622377 0.50090939 #> 3  Sepal.Width 0.4358663 0.13236114 #> 4 Sepal.Length 0.8280661 0.02401622 #>  #> Note: Variable importance was calculated using scaled coefficients (|a_j| * σ_j). #> Variable contributions can only be theoretically interpreted as such #> if the model was trained on scaled data. Scaling also changes the #> projection-pursuit optimization, which may affect the resulting tree. #> ------------------------------------- #> Confusion Matrix: #> TODO

title: Introduction to ppforest2
text: ppforest2 integrates tidymodels ecosystem via parsnip:
code: library(parsnip)  # Single tree spec <- pp_tree(penalty = 0.5) |>   set_engine("ppforest2") |>   set_mode("classification")  fit <- spec |> fit(Type ~ ., data = iris) predict(fit, iris[1:5, ]) #> # A tibble: 5 × 1 #>   .pred_class #>   <fct>       #> 1 setosa      #> 2 setosa      #> 3 setosa      #> 4 setosa      #> 5 setosa # Random forest spec <- pp_rand_forest(trees = 50, mtry = 2, penalty = 0.5) |>   set_engine("ppforest2") |>   set_mode("classification")  fit <- spec |> fit(Type ~ ., data = iris) predict(fit, iris[1:5, ], type = "prob") #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>   .pred_setosa .pred_versicolor .pred_virginica #>          <dbl>            <dbl>           <dbl> #> 1            1                0               0 #> 2            1                0               0 #> 3            1                0               0 #> 4            1                0               0 #> 5            1                0               0

title: Image Dataset — image
text: Contains  2310 observations instances 7 outdoor images Type 7 types outdoor images, brickface, cement,  foliage, grass, path, sky, window. X1 column center pixel region X2 row center pixel region. X3 number pixels region = 9. X4 results line extraction algorithm counts many lines length 5 (orientation) low contrast, less equal 5, go region. X5 measure contrast horizontally adjacent pixels region. 6, mean standard deviation given. attribute used vertical edge detector. X6 X5 sd X7 measures contrast vertically adjacent pixels. Used horizontal line detection. X8 sd X7 X9 average region (R + G + B)/3 X10 average region R value. X11 average region B value. X12 average region G value. X13 measure excess red: (2R - (G + B)) X14 measure excess blue: (2B - (G + R)) X15 measure excess green: (2G - (R + B)) X16 3-d nonlinear transformation RGB. (Algorithm can found Foley VanDam, Fundamentals Interactive Computer Graphics) X17 mean X16 X18 hue  mean

title: Plot a pprf model. — plot.pprf
text: Visualizes pprf model. default, shows variable importance one plot per metric side side. Use metric show single importance metric.

title: Plot a pprf model. — plot.pprf
code: # S3 method for class 'pprf' plot(x, type = "importance", metric = NULL, tree_index = 1L, node = 1L, ...)

title: Plot a pprf model. — plot.pprf
text: x pprf model. type Character string specifying plot type. "importance" (default) shows variable importance, "structure" shows specific tree embedded histograms, "projection" shows projected data node, "boundaries" shows decision boundaries specific tree. metric Character string selecting single importance metric plot: "projections", "weighted", "permuted".  NULL (default) shows available metrics side side separate panels.  used type = "importance". tree_index Integer index tree plot (1-based). used type = "structure", type = "projection", type = "boundaries". Defaults 1. node Integer index node projection plots. Defaults 1 (root). used type = "projection". ... Additional arguments passed internal plotting function.

title: Plot a pprf model. — plot.pprf
text: ggplot2 object (invisibly), NULL grid layout.

title: Plot a pprf model. — plot.pprf
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ forest <- pprf(Type ~ ., data = iris, size = 10) plot(forest)                                    # all metrics side by side plot(forest, metric = "permuted")               # single metric plot(forest, type = "structure", tree_index = 1) plot(forest, type = "projection", tree_index = 1) } # }

title: Plot a pptr model. — plot.pptr
text: Visualizes pptr model. default, shows mosaic overview tree structure, decision boundaries, variable importance. Use type show individual plots.

title: Plot a pptr model. — plot.pptr
code: # S3 method for class 'pptr' plot(x, type = NULL, metric = NULL, node = 1L, ...)

title: Plot a pptr model. — plot.pptr
text: x pptr model. type Character string specifying plot type. NULL (default) shows mosaic overview. options: "structure" tree embedded histograms, "importance" variable importance, "projection" projected data node, "boundaries" decision boundaries feature space. metric Character string selecting single importance metric plot: "projections", "weighted", "permuted" (availability depends model).  NULL (default) shows available metrics together.  used type = "importance". node Integer index node projection plots (1-based, breadth-first order). Defaults 1 (root node). used type = "projection". ... Additional arguments passed internal plotting function.

title: Plot a pptr model. — plot.pptr
text: ggplot2 object (invisibly), NULL mosaic layout.

title: Plot a pptr model. — plot.pptr
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ model <- pptr(Type ~ ., data = iris) plot(model)                         # mosaic overview plot(model, type = "structure")     # tree structure only plot(model, type = "importance")    # variable importance plot(model, type = "projection")   # projection histogram plot(model, type = "boundaries")   # decision boundaries } # }

what: Parsnip model specification for pprf. — pp_rand_forest
title: Parsnip model specification for pprf. — pp_rand_forest
text: Creates model specification Projection Pursuit random forest. Use set_engine("ppforest2") select ppforest2 engine.

title: Parsnip model specification for pprf. — pp_rand_forest
code: pp_rand_forest(   mode = "classification",   trees = NULL,   mtry = NULL,   penalty = NULL )

title: Parsnip model specification for pprf. — pp_rand_forest
text: mode character string model type. "classification" supported. trees number trees forest (maps size). mtry number variables consider split (maps n_vars). penalty regularization parameter (maps lambda).

title: Parsnip model specification for pprf. — pp_rand_forest
text: parsnip model specification.

title: Parsnip model specification for pprf. — pp_rand_forest
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(parsnip) spec <- pp_rand_forest(trees = 50, mtry = 2) %>% set_engine("ppforest2") fit <- spec %>% fit(Type ~ ., data = iris) predict(fit, iris) predict(fit, iris, type = "prob") } # }

what: Parsnip model specification for pptr. — pp_tree
title: Parsnip model specification for pptr. — pp_tree
text: Creates model specification single Projection Pursuit decision tree. Use set_engine("ppforest2") select ppforest2 engine.

title: Parsnip model specification for pptr. — pp_tree
code: pp_tree(mode = "classification", penalty = NULL)

title: Parsnip model specification for pptr. — pp_tree
text: mode character string model type. "classification" supported. penalty regularization parameter (maps lambda).

title: Parsnip model specification for pptr. — pp_tree
text: parsnip model specification.

title: Parsnip model specification for pptr. — pp_tree
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(parsnip) spec <- pp_tree(penalty = 0) %>% set_engine("ppforest2") fit <- spec %>% fit(Type ~ ., data = iris) predict(fit, iris) } # }

what: Trains a Random Forest of Project-Pursuit oblique decision trees. — pprf
title: Trains a Random Forest of Project-Pursuit oblique decision trees. — pprf
text: function trains Random Forest Project-Pursuit oblique decision tree using either formula data frame interface matrix-based interface. using formula interface, specify model formula data frame containing variables. matrix-based interface, provide matrices features labels directly. number trees controlled size parameter. tree trained stratified bootstrap sample drawn data. number variables consider split controlled n_vars parameter. lambda = 0, model trained using Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA). lambda > 0, model trained using Penalized Discriminant Analysis (PDA).

title: Trains a Random Forest of Project-Pursuit oblique decision trees. — pprf
code: pprf(   formula = NULL,   data = NULL,   x = NULL,   y = NULL,   size = 2,   lambda = 0,   n_vars = NULL,   p_vars = NULL,   seed = NULL,   n_threads = NULL )

title: Trains a Random Forest of Project-Pursuit oblique decision trees. — pprf
text: formula formula form y ~ x1 + x2 + ..., y vector labels x1, x2, ... features. data data frame containing variables formula. x matrix containing features observation. y matrix containing labels observation. size number trees forest. lambda regularization parameter. lambda = 0, model trained using Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA). lambda > 0, model trained using Penalized Discriminant Analysis (PDA). n_vars number variables consider split (integer). chosen uniformly split. default variables. used together p_vars. p_vars proportion variables consider split (number 0 1, exclusive). example, p_vars = 0.5 uses half features. used together n_vars. seed optional integer seed reproducibility. NULL (default), seed drawn R's RNG, set.seed() controls reproducibility. integer provided, value used directly. seed used training computing permuted variable importance. n_threads number threads use. default number cores available.

title: Trains a Random Forest of Project-Pursuit oblique decision trees. — pprf
text: pprf model trained x y.

title: Trains a Random Forest of Project-Pursuit oblique decision trees. — pprf
code: # Example 1: formula interface with the `iris` dataset pprf(Type ~ ., data = iris) #>  #> Random Forest of Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree #> ------------------------------------- #> Tree 1: #> If ([ 0.27 0.63 -0.69 -0.21 ] * x) < 1.063238: #>  If ([ 0.31 0.08 -0.48 -0.82 ] * x) < -1.619433: #>    Predict: virginica  #>  Else: #>    Predict: versicolor  #> Else: #>   Predict: setosa  #>  #> Tree 2: #> If ([ 0.23 0.72 -0.65 -0.03 ] * x) < 1.453875: #>  If ([ 0.21 0.49 -0.55 -0.64 ] * x) < -1.026834: #>    Predict: virginica  #>  Else: #>    Predict: versicolor  #> Else: #>   Predict: setosa  #>  #>   # Example 2: formula interface with the `iris` dataset with regularization pprf(Type ~ ., data = iris, lambda = 0.5) #>  #> Random Forest of Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree #> ------------------------------------- #> Tree 1: #> If ([ 0.08 -0.67 0.43 0.6 ] * x) < 0.2777239: #>   Predict: setosa  #> Else: #>  If ([ 0.02 0.05 -0.24 -0.97 ] * x) < -2.578496: #>    Predict: virginica  #>  Else: #>    Predict: versicolor  #>  #> Tree 2: #> If ([ 0.09 -0.68 0.46 0.56 ] * x) < 0.3777013: #>   Predict: setosa  #> Else: #>  If ([ 0.01 -0.15 0.33 0.93 ] * x) < 2.815652: #>    Predict: versicolor  #>  Else: #>    Predict: virginica  #>  #>   # Example 3: matrix interface with the `iris` dataset pprf(x = iris[, 1:4], y = iris[, 5]) #>  #> Random Forest of Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree #> ------------------------------------- #> Tree 1: #> If ([ 0.17 0.76 -0.56 -0.29 ] * x) < 1.249054: #>  If ([ 0.19 0.33 -0.31 -0.87 ] * x) < -0.8064449: #>    Predict: virginica  #>  Else: #>    Predict: versicolor  #> Else: #>   Predict: setosa  #>  #> Tree 2: #> If ([ 0.05 -0.79 0.59 0.17 ] * x) < -0.2461427: #>   Predict: setosa  #> Else: #>  If ([ 0.12 0.42 -0.27 -0.86 ] * x) < -0.8229396: #>    Predict: virginica  #>  Else: #>    Predict: versicolor  #>  #>   # Example 4: matrix interface with the `iris` dataset with regularization pprf(x = iris[, 1:4], y = iris[, 5], lambda = 0.5) #>  #> Random Forest of Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree #> ------------------------------------- #> Tree 1: #> If ([ 0.08 -0.71 0.47 0.51 ] * x) < 0.1726278: #>   Predict: setosa  #> Else: #>  If ([ 0.01 0.23 -0.47 -0.85 ] * x) < -3.015483: #>    Predict: virginica  #>  Else: #>    Predict: versicolor  #>  #> Tree 2: #> If ([ 0.11 -0.72 0.43 0.53 ] * x) < 0.2246059: #>   Predict: setosa  #> Else: #>  If ([ 0.07 0.19 -0.38 -0.91 ] * x) < -2.423834: #>    Predict: virginica  #>  Else: #>    Predict: versicolor  #>  #>   # Example 5: formula interface with the `crabs` dataset pprf(Type ~ . - sex + as.numeric(as.factor(sex)), data = crabs) #>  #> Random Forest of Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree #> ------------------------------------- #> Tree 1: #> If ([ 0.5 0.27 0 -0.45 0.43 0.53 ] * x) < 1.608614: #>   Predict: B  #> Else: #>   Predict: O  #>  #> Tree 2: #> If ([ 0.63 0.18 0.04 -0.53 0.49 0.22 ] * x) < 1.145166: #>   Predict: B  #> Else: #>   Predict: O  #>  #>   # Example 6: formula interface with the `crabs` dataset with regularization pprf(Type ~ . - sex + as.numeric(as.factor(sex)), data = crabs, lambda = 0.5) #>  #> Random Forest of Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree #> ------------------------------------- #> Tree 1: #> If ([ 0.61 0.25 0.01 -0.09 0.55 -0.5 ] * x) < 16.8111: #>   Predict: B  #> Else: #>   Predict: O  #>  #> Tree 2: #> If ([ 0.73 0.21 0 -0.12 0.56 -0.31 ] * x) < 17.04963: #>   Predict: B  #> Else: #>   Predict: O  #>  #>   # Example 7: matrix interface with the `crabs` dataset x <- crabs[, 2:5] x$sex <- as.numeric(as.factor(crabs$sex)) pprf(x = x, y = crabs$Type) #>  #> Random Forest of Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree #> ------------------------------------- #> Tree 1: #> If ([ 0.63 0.25 0.25 -0.55 0.42 ] * x) < 1.573025: #>   Predict: B  #> Else: #>   Predict: O  #>  #> Tree 2: #> If ([ 0.72 0.18 0.24 -0.56 0.28 ] * x) < 1.340618: #>   Predict: B  #> Else: #>   Predict: O  #>  #>   # Example 8: matrix interface with the `crabs` dataset with regularization x <- crabs[, 2:5] x$sex <- as.numeric(as.factor(crabs$sex)) pprf(x = x, y = crabs$Type, lambda = 0.5) #>  #> Random Forest of Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree #> ------------------------------------- #> Tree 1: #> If ([ 0.81 0.38 0.04 -0.09 0.43 ] * x) < 16.06476: #>   Predict: B  #> Else: #>   Predict: O  #>  #> Tree 2: #> If ([ 0.53 0.2 0.03 -0.06 0.83 ] * x) < 10.87641: #>   Predict: B  #> Else: #>   Predict: O  #>  #>

what: Trains a Project-Pursuit oblique decision tree. — pptr
title: Trains a Project-Pursuit oblique decision tree. — pptr
text: function trains Project-Pursuit oblique decision tree using either formula data frame interface matrix-based interface. using formula interface, specify model formula data frame containing variables. matrix-based interface, provide matrices features labels directly. lambda = 0, model trained using Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA). lambda > 0, model trained using Penalized Discriminant Analysis (PDA).

title: Trains a Project-Pursuit oblique decision tree. — pptr
code: pptr(formula = NULL, data = NULL, x = NULL, y = NULL, lambda = 0, seed = NULL)

title: Trains a Project-Pursuit oblique decision tree. — pptr
text: formula formula form y ~ x1 + x2 + ..., y vector labels x1, x2, ... features. data data frame containing variables formula. x matrix containing features observation. y matrix containing labels observation. lambda regularization parameter. lambda = 0, model trained using Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA). lambda > 0, model trained using Penalized Discriminant Analysis (PDA). seed optional integer seed reproducibility. NULL (default), seed drawn R's RNG, set.seed() controls reproducibility. integer provided, value used directly.

title: Trains a Project-Pursuit oblique decision tree. — pptr
text: pptr model trained x y.

title: Trains a Project-Pursuit oblique decision tree. — pptr
code: # Example 1: formula interface with the `iris` dataset pptr(Type ~ ., data = iris) #>  #> Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree: #> If ([ 0.19 0.71 -0.66 -0.17 ] * x) < 1.070804: #>  If ([ 0.23 0.36 -0.44 -0.79 ] * x) < -1.062904: #>    Predict: virginica  #>  Else: #>    Predict: versicolor  #> Else: #>   Predict: setosa  #>   # Example 2: formula interface with the `iris` dataset with regularization pptr(Type ~ ., data = iris, lambda = 0.5) #>  #> Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree: #> If ([ 0.07 -0.68 0.45 0.58 ] * x) < 0.2732373: #>   Predict: setosa  #> Else: #>  If ([ 0.01 0.16 -0.34 -0.93 ] * x) < -2.714733: #>    Predict: virginica  #>  Else: #>    Predict: versicolor  #>   # Example 3: matrix interface with the `iris` dataset pptr(x = iris[, 1:4], y = iris[, 5]) #> If ([ 0.19 0.71 -0.66 -0.17 ] * x) < 1.070804: #>  If ([ 0.23 0.36 -0.44 -0.79 ] * x) < -1.062904: #>    Predict: virginica  #>  Else: #>    Predict: versicolor  #> Else: #>   Predict: setosa  #>   # Example 4: matrix interface with the `iris` dataset with regularization pptr(x = iris[, 1:4], y = iris[, 5], lambda = 0.5) #> If ([ 0.07 -0.68 0.45 0.58 ] * x) < 0.2732373: #>   Predict: setosa  #> Else: #>  If ([ 0.01 0.16 -0.34 -0.93 ] * x) < -2.714733: #>    Predict: virginica  #>  Else: #>    Predict: versicolor  #>   # Example 5: formula interface with the `crabs` dataset pptr(Type ~ . - sex + as.numeric(as.factor(sex)), data = crabs) #>  #> Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree: #> If ([ 0.59 0.17 0.09 -0.55 0.48 0.29 ] * x) < 1.136014: #>   Predict: B  #> Else: #>   Predict: O  #>   # Example 6: formula interface with the `crabs` dataset with regularization pptr(Type ~ . - sex + as.numeric(as.factor(sex)), data = crabs, lambda = 0.5) #>  #> Project-Pursuit Oblique Decision Tree: #> If ([ 0.71 0.25 0 -0.13 0.65 -0.04 ] * x) < 18.34709: #>   Predict: B  #> Else: #>   Predict: O  #>   # Example 7: matrix interface with the `crabs` dataset x <- crabs[, 2:5] x$sex <- as.numeric(as.factor(crabs$sex)) pptr(x = x, y = crabs$Type) #> If ([ 0.67 0.16 0.31 -0.59 0.27 ] * x) < 1.40659: #>   Predict: B  #> Else: #>   Predict: O  #>   # Example 8: matrix interface with the `crabs` dataset with regularization x <- crabs[, 2:5] x$sex <- as.numeric(as.factor(crabs$sex)) pptr(x = x, y = crabs$Type, lambda = 0.5) #> If ([ 0.89 0.41 0.06 -0.08 0.17 ] * x) < 18.43002: #>   Predict: B  #> Else: #>   Predict: O  #>

what: Predicts the labels or vote proportions of a set of observations using a pprf model. — predict.pprf
title: Predicts the labels or vote proportions of a set of observations using a pprf model. — predict.pprf
text: Predicts labels vote proportions set observations using pprf model.

title: Predicts the labels or vote proportions of a set of observations using a pprf model. — predict.pprf
code: # S3 method for class 'pprf' predict(object, new_data = NULL, type = "class", ...)

title: Predicts the labels or vote proportions of a set of observations using a pprf model. — predict.pprf
text: object pprf model. new_data data frame matrix new observations predict. NULL, first positional argument ... used backward compatibility. type type prediction: "class" (default) returns factor predicted labels, "prob" returns data frame vote proportions. ... backward compatibility, first positional argument treated new_data new_data NULL.

title: Predicts the labels or vote proportions of a set of observations using a pprf model. — predict.pprf

Scaling also changes the #> projection-pursuit optimization, which may affect the resulting tree. #> ------------------------------------- #> Confusion Matrix: #> TODO

title: ppforest2 0.1.0
text: Core: Projection-pursuit oblique decision trees random forests classification, using LDA/PDA optimization. Core: Random uniform variable selection per split forest diversity. Core: Three variable importance measures: permuted (VI1), projections (VI2), weighted projections (VI3). Core: --bag error estimation forests. Core: OpenMP multi-threaded forest training. Core: JSON serialization trained models. Core: Cross-platform reproducibility: identical results seed Linux, macOS, Windows. R: pptr() pprf() formula matrix interfaces. R: predict() class labels (type = "class") vote proportions (type = "prob"). R: save_json() load_json() model persistence. R: tidymodels/parsnip integration: pp_tree() pp_rand_forest() model specifications. R: ggplot2 visualizations: tree diagrams, variable importance plots, projection histograms, decision boundary plots. R: Bundled datasets: crab, crabs, fishcatch, glass, image, iris, leukemia, lymphoma, NCI60, olive, parkinson, wine. CLI: train: fit tree forest CSV, save JSON. CLI: predict: classify new data saved model. CLI: evaluate: train/test evaluation smart convergence. CLI: benchmark: multi-scenario performance benchmarks baseline comparison.
